coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor prepared by silica-based fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer for efficient detection of bisphenol A
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Huanran
Wang, Peng
Bai, Hao
Shao, Yanming
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and accurate bisphenol A (BPA) detection is becoming increasingly important in many sectors. We developed a simple molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) ratiometric fluorescence sensor to efficiently detect BPA by combining red fluorescence SiO2@CdTe@MIP with blue fluorescence carbon dots (CDs). The addition of BPA to the sensor will cause a rapid decline in the fluorescence intensity of CdTe at 625 nm, while the signal of CDs at 405 nm remains constant, leading to a change in the fluorescence color. A good linear relationship exists between the fluorescence intensity ratio of [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0 and concentrations of BPA ranging from 1 to 100 μM with a minimum detection limit of 0.4 μM. Meanwhile, the prepared sensor shows remarkable efficacy in analyzing BPA, with more consistent average recoveries ranging from 95.6 % to 110.9 % and smaller deviations of 4.7 % in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenol A
Molecularly imprinted
Fluorescence
Nanoparticles
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of SiO2 (a, d), SiO2@CdTe@MIP (b, e), and SiO2@CdTe@NIP (c, f). (B) FT-IR spectra of CdTe (a), SiO2 (b), and SiO2@CdTe@MIP (c).

(A) The effect of incubation time. (B) Fluorescence analysis of BPA by CDs/SiO2@CdTe@MIP (a), CDs/SiO2@CdTe@NIP (b), and SiO2@CdTe@MIP (c). Linear relationship between [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0 and BPA concentrations (d). (C) Selectivity of the sensor. (D) Fluorescence quenching based on electron transfer (a) and molecular orbital theory (b).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor prepared by silica-based fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer for efficient detection of bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Research Center of Smart Sensors, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of High-Orbits-Electron Materials and Protection Technology for Aerospace, School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, Xidian University, Shaanxi 710126, People’s Republic of China

School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Research Center of Smart Sensors, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of High-Orbits-Electron Materials and Protection Technology for Aerospace, School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, Xidian University, Shaanxi 710126, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710021, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710021, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and accurate bisphenol A (BPA) detection is becoming increasingly important in many sectors. We developed a simple molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) ratiometric fluorescence sensor to efficiently detect BPA by combining red fluorescence SiO2@CdTe@MIP with blue fluorescence carbon dots (CDs). The addition of BPA to the sensor will cause a rapid decline in the fluorescence intensity of CdTe at 625 nm, while the signal of CDs at 405 nm remains constant, leading to a change in the fluorescence color. A good linear relationship exists between the fluorescence intensity ratio of [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0 and concentrations of BPA ranging from 1 to 100 μM with a minimum detection limit of 0.4 μM. Meanwhile, the prepared sensor shows remarkable efficacy in analyzing BPA, with more consistent average recoveries ranging from 95.6 % to 110.9 % and smaller deviations of 4.7 % in water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is an industrial chemical commonly used in epoxy resins and polycarbonate plastics. However, ubiquitous BPA exposure from the widespread use of BPA-containing products causes irreparable damage to the environment and human health. Additionally, BPA is an endocrine disruptor with substantial adverse effects on human reproductive health [1]. Therefore, developing a rapid and accurate detection technique is essential for detecting BPA in real samples. Fluorescence analysis for compound detection has become common because of its low cost and simple operation. Consequently, quantum dots (QDs) have been extensively applied in fluorescence analysis due to their exceptional optical properties and chemical stability [2]. Additionally, the molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors can accurately detect the target molecule based on the fluorescence signal of QDs, combined with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which can selectively bind target molecules in a complex matrix. Furthermore, ratiometric fluorescence sensing can be used by changing the ratio of two kinds of fluorescence signals to eliminate the interference of environmental factors and achieve the visual detection effect [3]. On the other hand, a mixing strategy was used to construct a hybrid sensor to streamline fluorescence color regulation, bypass the tedious preparation process of traditional core–shell ratiometric fluorescence sensors [4].

Herein, we developed a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor by mixing CDs and SiO2@CdTe@MIP to detect BPA efficiently. The CDs were used as a reference signal in the ratiometric fluorescent system. The results of the fluorescence experiment indicated that the sensor exhibited exceptional detection capabilities and successfully identified BPA in water samples.

Fig. 1 displays the preparation procedure for the sensor. Silicon dioxide (SiO2) was initially prepared as a carrier for MIPs. Next, CdTe, BPA, and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) were stirred to form a ligand complex, and SiO2 was subsequently dispersed. Then, TEOS was cross-linked on the surface of SiO2 to obtain MIPs (SiO2@CdTe@MIP). The non-imprinted polymer (SiO2@CdTe@NIP) was prepared using the same procedure without adding BPA. A certain amount of CDs was mixed with the MIPs to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

The TEM images (Fig. 2 A a and d) show that SiO2 has good monodispersity with a regular spherical shape and a diameter of 180 nm. After the imprinted layer was formed, the particle size of SiO2@CdTe@MIP increased to approximately 220 nm (Fig. 2A b and e). Furthermore, the SiO2@CdTe@MIP and SiO2@CdTe@NIP (Fig. 2A e and f) had similar morphologies. Fig. 2B displays the FT-IR spectra of materials. The absorption peaks at 1,557 and 1,338 cm−1 can be ascribed to the symmetrical and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO– in thioglycolic acid (TGA), indicating the successful preparation of TGA-modified CdTe (Fig. 2B a). The peaks at 801 and 1,091 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O, confirming the successful preparation of SiO2 (Fig. 2B b). After imprited, the peaks of benzene rings in BPA appeared at 1,600 and 1,460 cm−1, and the peaks at 2,925 and 2,855 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration peak of –CH2 in APTES (Fig. 2B c) [5]. In the XRD pattern of the CdTe, the peaks at 2θ = 24°, 41°, and 47°- demonstrate that the CdTe have a typical cubic sphalerite crystal structure (Fig.S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the as-prepared sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of CDs in the presence of BPA and its structural analogs was measured to investigate if CDs could be used as reference signals. The fluorescence intensity of the solution remained constant after incubation with BPA and its analogs (Fig. S2). The as-prepared CDs and CdTe revealed single fluorescence emission peaks at 405 and 605 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). When CDs were blended with MIPs in a particular proportion, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor was obtained, indicating CDs could be used as a reference signal in detecting BPA. Meanwhile, good sensor stability was demonstrated by the well-maintained relative fluorescence intensity after being stored for seven days (Fig. S4). Additionally, the incubation period was optimized. When MIPs bound to BPA, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe decreased rapidly within 1 min, followed by a gradual decline until it remained relatively stable at 10 min (Fig. 3 A). This can be attributed to the change in the number of imprinted sites on the surface of MIPs. Initially, the uptake of BPA led to a rapid reduction in the number of imprinted sites, which stabilized after 10 min.

The sensitivity of the sensor was investigated by comparing the quenching degree of MIPs and NIPs with various concentrations of BPA. When no BPA was added, two independent emission peaks attributed to CDs and CdTe appeared (Fig. 3B a). The red fluorescence of CdTe was gradually quenched as BPA concentrations increased. However, the blue fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged, increasing the fluorescence intensity ratio [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0. The fluorescent color of the detection solution changed gradually with the increase in the concentration of BPA (inset of Fig. 3Ba). A linear relationship was observed between the concentrations of BPA and [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0, which satisfied the linear equation of y = 0.02181x + 1.0337 (R2 = 0.9960) (Fig. 3B d). Furthermore, the detection limit was 0.4 μM. For CDs/SiO2@CdTe@NIP, the fluorescence intensity at 625 nm was just slightly decreased as the concentration of BPA increased (Fig. 3B b). The change of [I405/I625]/[I405/I625]0 was inconspicuous, and the color change of the mixed solution was difficult to distinguish with the naked eye (inset of Fig. 3B b). Additionally, BPA was detected by MIPs in the absence of CDs. The fluorescence intensity at 625 nm decreased gradually as the BPA concentration increased (Fig. 3B c). However, the color changes of the detection mixture are difficult to observe because of the absence of a reference signal. Furthermore, TBBPA, BIP, and BP were selected as structural analogs of BPA to investigate the specific recognition ability of the sensor. In comparison to the slight fluorescence quenching observed in the presence of TBBPA, BIP, and BP, considerable quenching was found in the presence of BPA (Fig. 3C). The selectivity was caused by the presence of imprinted sites, whose size and interaction closely matched with the BPA.

Fig. 3D depicts the quenching mechanism of the sensor. When BPA was combined with the MIPs, a complex was formed between the electron-rich –OH connected to the aromatic ring and the electron-deficient amino group on the surface of CdTe due to a strong hydrogen bonding interaction. Therefore, the energy excited by CdTe was transferred to the complex, causing fluorescence quenching. Notably, there is no spectra overlap between the UV absorption spectra of BPA and the fluorescence spectra of MIPs [6]. The quenching mechanism can be explained based on molecular orbital theory. A fluorescent signal was generated when CdTe was excited by UV light. When BPA bound with the MIPs, an interaction was observed between the amino group on the surface of the CdTe and the hydroxyl group of the BPA due to hydrogen bonding, which resulted in the electron transfer between the BPA and the CdTe. Due to the higher energy level of the formed complex than that of BPA, the electrons of the CdTe, which were excited again, could be directly transferred from the conduction band of MIPs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the formed complex. Therefore, no fluorescent signal was generated by CdTe after BPA was bounded with MIPs. Based on the analysis mentioned above, the quenching mechanism of the prepared sensor is photo-induced electron transfer.

The feasibility of this sensor in practical applications was also investigated. Three different concentrations of BPA were added to water samples for spiked experiments. Table S1 demonstrates that the recoveries of BPA in tap and lake water ranged from 96.0 %–110.9 % and 95.6 %–108.2 %, respectively, with RSDs < 4.7 % (n = 3). These results suggest that the method can be used to analyze BPA water samples. Furthermore, the established method was compared with previously reported methods for BPA (Table S2). Although our method has a relatively high LOD for BPA, the simple preparation process and easy regulation of the fluorescent color endow this hybrid sensor with substantial potential for utilizing it to detect low-concentration pollutants.

A hybrid molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed using a simple mixed method to detect BPA. This time-saving strategy can significantly shorten and simplify the preparation processes compared to the traditional molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which were prepared using the multi-step coating method. The sensor exhibits the selectivity of MIPs and the sensitivity of QDs. Additionally, fluorescence experiments show that the prepared sensor has a good fluorescence response for BPA.

Huanran Feng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Peng Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Hao Bai: Investigation, Data curation. Yanming Shao: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22005234, 21806097).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136490.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Chim. Acta 1260 (2023) 341174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular Imprinting Based Hybrid Ratiometric Fluorescence Sensor for the Visual Determination of Bovine Hemoglobin

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of ternary-emission molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor based on metal–organic framework for visual detection of chloramphenicol

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Imprinted ratiometric fluorescence capillary sensor based on UiO-66-NH2 for rapid determination of sialic acid
